planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring in moist, humus-rich, neutral to slightly alkaline woodland soil in partial to full shade; Asian twinleaf is more persistent through summer than its North American counterpart (J. diphylla).

directions: Plant divisions in early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; Asian twinleaf prefers the same leaf-mold rich, shaded planting conditions used for trilliums and bloodroot. Jeffersonia dubia is the Asian counterpart to the North American twinleaf, native to woodland and scrub in Manchuria and Korea, producing kidney-shaped leaves with blue-green to purple-flushed coloring alongside lavender-blue cup-shaped flowers in early spring. Unlike the North American species, J. dubia retains its attractive foliage well into fall and is slow-growing but exceptionally long-lived.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in full shade; scoliopus is a California coastal woodland specialist requiring cool, shaded, consistently moist conditions similar to its native redwood forest habitat.

directions: Plant divisions in early spring in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in full shade; scoliopus must have consistently moist, cool conditions and summer-dry stress will cause premature dormancy or death. Scoliopus bigelovii is one of California's most unusual native woodland perennials, producing mottled strap-like leaves in winter and bearing small brownish-purple veined flowers with a fetid odor from January through March, attracting fungus gnats for pollination. It is native to cool, moist, shaded canyons and redwood forest understories along the California coast and goes dormant by late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, alkaline to neutral woodland soil in full shade; ALL PARTS ARE TOXIC and should not be grown where children or pets have unsupervised access.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full shade, replicating its natural habitat of base-rich European deciduous woodland; herb Paris dislikes acidic conditions and resents disturbance. Paris quadrifolia is a singularly distinctive European woodland perennial producing an unbranched stem topped with a precise whorl of exactly four broad leaves and a single greenish-yellow flower in late spring, followed by a glossy black berry. The entire plant is poisonous (containing steroidal saponins) and should be handled with care; it is slow to multiply but an elegant collector's plant for shaded woodland gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring in cool, moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in full shade sheltered from wind; glaucidium absolutely must not be exposed to drying winds, drought, or summer heat.

directions: Plant divisions in early spring in cool, moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in full shade sheltered from wind; glaucidium requires consistently moist woodland conditions and will fail quickly in dry soil, strong sun, or heat. Glaucidium palmatum is a monotypic Japanese woodland perennial of singular beauty, producing large, deeply palmate, maple-like leaves and exquisite single mauve-to-lavender poppy-like flowers 2-3 inches across with prominent golden stamens in May. It is slow to establish and resents disturbance; established plants are long-lived and virtually pest-free in appropriate cool woodland conditions in the Pacific Northwest, upper Midwest, and New England.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in full shade beneath conifers; queen's cup requires cool summer temperatures and the moist conditions of its native montane coniferous forest understory.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in full shade, ideally beneath conifers where needle duff provides natural mulch; queen's cup needs cool, moist conditions year-round and will decline in hot, dry summers regardless of supplemental watering. Clintonia uniflora is a low-growing western North American native of cool, moist coniferous forest understories from Alaska to California and east to Montana, producing 2-3 broad, glossy, basal leaves and a single large, six-petaled white flower in late spring, followed by a single brilliant metallic-blue berry. It spreads slowly by rhizomes to form small colonies and is an exquisite native wildflower for cool, shaded Pacific Northwest and Rocky Mountain gardens.
